sentences.text: In the kitchen.
Well, when we were not picked by the manufacturers, you know, we really thought we would be sent back to Auschwitz.
We were pretty sure of that.
If nobody needed us, you know, they were not going to just let us run around, take showers, and pick berries.
But they picked us to work in the kitchen.
These three women had taken all of the people that was left behind to work in the kitchen.
And I wound up being one of the people to be sent outside to take the meals, the new meal, to the Germans in their look-out towers.
And I had a very horrible incident happen to me one day while I was outside.
One of the reasons they picked me is because they knew I had a sister in the camp.
And even though I was outside the gate, I wouldn't try to run away, because of my sister in the camp.
Not that there was any place to do-- go, but it was very high grass.
The grass was like, you know, above my knee that I was walking in.
And I, of course, entertained the thought, what if I lie down someplace in the grass?
But I knew that those guys are up there in the lookout towers.
But it was a fantasy to entertain, anyhow.
And this one day, as I climbed up in the lookout tower, and I never knew which of the Germans I was going to see.
After awhile, I got to know their faces.
And they got to know my face.
I had to wait for them to finish and send back the dishes.
I took like one of these things that had like four compartments in it, and then I put all the empty ones back into this carry-all, and I carry it back.
And sometimes they left some food in it.
And I could eat it, you know, which was very, you know, much appreciated, to say the least.
But this one particular time, I was sitting with my legs hanging out of the lookout tower, and my back towards the soldier who was eating inside the lookout tower.
And he started a conversation with me.
And he said, where are you from?
I said, I'm Hungarian.
And he said, how come you speak German?
And I said, well, I've studied a little bit of a German in school.
And so I learned here.
And he said, what about your father?
Where is your father?
And I said, my father is in the army.
I didn't say a work camp.
I said my father is an officer in the army.
He said, the Hungarian army?
And I said, that's right.
So I thought he was now interested in me, because, you know, my father wasn't that different than him.
And I was relaxed and at ease.
And suddenly, I sense him coming up in back of me.
And I turned to look at him.
And there was a horrible look in his eye, like a glee in his eye.
And I got scared.
I realized that he didn't have anything good in his mind.
[DRINKS] And how old was he about?
He was probably late '20s, early '30s.
Oh, he even told me he's a father too, and he has children, and all this.
So I was totally relaxed.
But then when I saw that look on his face, I just kind of jumped up and into the-- into the little lookout tower, because I was hanging out.
But I somehow pushed myself, I guess, on my hip to turn around, and I gave him a shove.
And I started running down the stairs, but I fell.
And I must have hit a rock, because I knocked out one of my tooth.
And I didn't know it at that time, but I saw blood coming from my mouth, you know?
And you think I run back?
I waited for the dishes, because I was afraid to come back for-- you know, without the dishes.
I've waited for the dishes.
And then when I got to the gate, and the other two guys at the gate, the two guards saw me bleeding and everything.
And oh, and I was all, you know, scuffed and bleeding.
My uniform was torn.
They got a kind of kick out of it.
They kind of like laughed, you know, like what happened to her?
You know, that kind of thing.
And they let me through the gate.
And I went in the kitchen.

And the two older women who were the chefs, who were women from among us-- as a matter of fact, Frau Rubencheck and Frau Hoffman were two older women who figured out right away-- they each had a daughter with my age-- that if we are this many people at the camp-- you see, you have to be older and experienced to think properly-- that they'll probably need chefs.
Now, there were no more chefs.
One of them was a famous opera star, Marta Hoffman.
And she always sang opera in the kitchen.
And the Germans loved it.
And they said-- they went to volunteer to the German [? women, ?] knocking on the window in the kitchen.
And they said, they're chefs from back home.
Do they need any chefs?
Well, they could cook, but they certainly weren't chefs.
And they were hired.
So when they saw me come into the kitchen, they said, we must hide every trace of this, because we can't make anything of it, or they'll take her away, meaning me, and all this sort of stuff.
So they tried to clean me up the best I could, but I did lose a tooth.
And, you know, we just were told, which I-- just not talk about, not make anything.
Because any incident like this that happened there, there was some kind of evidence, you know, of them, their wrongdoing, or whatever, they just took you away.
So we were in this place where I was able to steal some bread, even for my friends at night, before I left the kitchen to go to my barrack.
You know, my barracks were full of these people from the factories who were so hungry.
I could even steal some bread or save my own ration, because I ate other things in the kitchen.
Like, my sister would save me some vegetables and things from the kitchen, you know, where she was-- where she was peeling.
And we would bring the bones from the kitchen, because now this was the meals for the Germans.
We got the peels in our soup.
And we would bring the bones.
And then they would eat the marrow from the bones and little gristle that was left on, and so forth.
So we'd be exchanging food all the time.
So very often, we did not eat our own bread, and stole some when we could, and bring it to the people in our barracks, you know, that we're starving all the time.
These women, the three women.
Actually, just two, Lucy and Emma.
The other one went with the workers to the factories.
Oh, no.
They-- we would not let them see us.
But I tell you something.
I can't say they were nice, and I was never beaten by them.
And as I said, one was actually a giggler, and the other was always smiling, which was uncanny.
But the two chefs, the two ladies, who they find out later on was no chefs, you know.
As a matter of fact, they knew right away that she was a professional opera singer, Marta, as soon as she open her mouth, you know, and they liked her very much to sing.
They had some kind of a relationship with them.
As a matter of-- oh, yes.
As a matter of fact, they have found out that the head woman, Marta, had a birthday, and they've been saving from the rations to make a party for her with a cake and everything.
And some of the kids, like myself, were asked to entertain.
And I did the Csardas with my sister and a couple of kids.
And we put on a little show for them, after the men were all, you know, tucked away so they were no place around.
So it was just the two women in the kitchen.
They were appreciative of the fact that these women were decent.
I mean, they, you know, treated us more or less like they would treat workers, rather than, you know, dirty Jews, or prisoners, and that they had some sense of humanity in them.
And they looked at these two older women as being, you know, somebody, even though they're in this situation, and looked at us as children.
So we were very appreciative of that, you know?
And--
We were in Christianstadt from September till the very beginning of January.
The very first of the year, they put us on the road.
And we were too much, because this was already the January of 1945.
And the Russians were approaching.
So the Germans were trying to evacuate the camps so the Russians wouldn't find us.
And they were marching all the people from these different camps towards Bergen-Belsen.
Because we had no idea where we were going.
But we were given some warm clothes.
They got some clothes, I guess from Auschwitz, or one of the camps, where people had left their clothing, you know, on arrival.
Because most of it went to the Germans.
But some clothing had arrived.
And we were given some coats, and shoes, and stuff like that.
In Christianstadt before they set us out in January on these roads with this much snow.
But I had wooden shoes.
I didn't get a pair of regular shoes.
I had wooden shoes in which you cannot walk on the snow.
I found that out very quickly, because the snow sticks to it.
And it was like walking on stilts.
I don't remember.
But I know we used paper and whatever scraps we could find to wind around our shoes, especially the ones like myself who had wooden shoes.
Because they kept falling off, the clogs.
So we were putting, you know, whatever-- hay, if we go to the barn, we would get some hay and wrap it around our feet--
--and put it on.
Right.
Germany.
And then to Bergen-Belsen.
And it was a six week march.
And over 2,000 of us left Christianstadt.
And about 400, approximately, a few less, few more, had arrived, actually, in Bergen-Belsen.
All the rest of them died on the road, either from frost, or starvation, or they simply just gave up, which is what I wanted to do several times.
I just pleaded to my sister, let me sit down, and let them shoot me.
Because whoever couldn't keep up with the march, they would just be shot and left there, left on the road, on the side of the road.
And I would say, I'm tired.
I just don't want to go any further.
I'm hungry.
I'm freezing.
I'm sick.
I just want to die.
Oh, no.
She wouldn't let me die.
She made up all kinds of stories.
Tonight, we'll probably find a very nice barn, because that's what they tried to do.
They tried to-- they walked us on country roads.
And they tried to find a barn to put us up.
And then they would take the house.
They would take the farmer's house, and have hot baths, and so forth, and so on, and a good cooked meal, while we were in the barn.
And in the morning, we wouldn't get anything that night.
We would arrive there just, you know, absolutely dead.
But in the morning, the farmers would cook up some potatoes, or turnips, whatever they had.
And we would get one or two potatoes, you know, to send us on our way for another day's march.
So that's why I said to my sister, forget it.
Just let me sit down.
I even talked again to myself, that if I was shot, and there would be all this blood, like I saw the others bleeding, that the blood would be warm.
And I would be warm.
So I really just wanted to die.
I said, just leave me alone.
No, no, no.
If you die, how am I going to face Mother?
I promised her I would take care of you.
And I'm going to do it, she said.
With my last breath, you going to have to come along.
And she dragged me.
And it was 1945.
This is the spring-- I mean, winter of 1945, January.
We marched all of-- well, no.
Actually, we left, I guess, the second week in January.
No.
We left right-- right the first week in January.
We left Christianstadt.
And we were on the road all of January.
I was 15 years old.
And we walked in the snow.
And looking back, there's no way on this earth that I could ever explain why I was the one that survived, because as I said, I was always the skinny little kid, never too strong.
But I did gain back, I would say, most of the weight I lost in Auschwitz.
I gained it back in Christianstadt, but lost it again, of course, on the road.
And somehow I kept going.
And that's why, I said before, that I had come to the conclusion that tradition is a very, very important thing in our life.
Because I felt a sense of belonging to a family.
And I kept saying to myself, it can't last forever.
This was in the moments that I wasn't so totally depressed and wanted to die.
And that this war would be over, and when the war would be over, I would go home.
And I would be with mother, and father, and with the children, the two little ones, and all this.
And there was, you know, another voice telling me, but your mother is dead.
The two little ones are dead.
But I wasn't listening.
I wasn't listening, because I didn't believe that my mother was dead.
And looking back, I know that that was my way to survive, thinking that they would be there.
Because if I had admitted to myself that mother would not be there, I am sure I would not have survived.
Because that's what kept me going.
When we finally arrived in Bergen-Belsen in March, and by that time, [DRINKS] things was so bad there.
Well, they were really-- they were really following orders.
They were scared to death with all these Germans who took over their house, kicked them out of their beds, took their food, you know, made them heat water for them to bathe, and everything.
And then in one of the particular farmer's house, somehow my sister got in the kitchen.
She was very, very brave.
She did somethings that was so brave, it was unbelievable.
She got into the kitchen.
And she said, do you have any warm water for us?
And the woman said, well, if, you know, there's any left, you can come in and wash.
And I'm sure that was the water the Germans already washed in.
Because it did not look clean.
But we weren't particular.
And we took off our dress.
Well, I just tied mine halfway down here, you know, kind of rolled it up.
And we were so skinny.
I could just twist it.
You know, I was probably this narrow by that time.
And I leaned over the water, naked, trying to wash myself.
And one of the Germans came from another room and found me the kitchen, opened the door, and gave me a kick in my behind, and threw me out into the cold, with, you know, just naked.
You know, let me pull down my clothes and grab the clothes from the floor, you know, and ran out.
But the farmers, basically, I think they would have liked to help us, I really do.
Because we walked through Czechoslovakia, Carlsbad.
And that's a place that used be a resort, Carlsbad, where people went for their baths.
And there was a lot of red salt, I remember, on the road.
And the prisoners were picking up this red salt and mixing it with snow, and eating it, because it was something to put in your stomach.
So--
Yeah, so the death march was six weeks.
We were into March.
And by the time we arrived to Bergen-Belsen, the 400 of us, what we saw there was worse than Auschwitz.
We never knew that anything be could worse.
But by this time, the Germans really didn't give a damn.
They didn't bury-- well, they didn't bury the bodies.
The bodies were piled up like sky high.
When somebody died, they had these little like wheel barrows, and they had the people who could still walk and carry, lift up these bodies, and not all of them completely dead either.
And they put them in these wheel barrows.
And they had to wheel them behind the barracks, and put them in these big piles.
And believe me, many times I looked at those piles, like when you see a dead animal on the road, and you say to yourself while you're driving, look straight ahead.
Don't look at it, because it's ugly, you know.
And your eye can't leave it.
That's what happened to me.
And I would look to these piles of corpses.
And sometimes I saw people moving within the pile.
And the smell from the decaying bodies, because the snow was melting.
It was March.
And sunny days, and as the sun was melting the snow.
And the bodies were decaying.
The stink was absolutely unbearable.
And now we were put in a barrack that didn't have any [INAUDIBLE] as they called it, these bunk beds.
It was just the floor.
And we were all like on top of each other, just lying on the floor.
That's how we were.
In Bergen-Belsen.
This was the end.
And then they didn't really care to do anything, you know, according to their prior rules.
They just kind of let things go, because the Germans were really losing the war by March of 1945.
They still called the appels, yes.
They did do that.
And they still gave us that one meal.
But there were many, many horrible incidences.
Because the food was so scarce, that-- that as they would bring this bucket of soup to distribute it.
And we were all lined up with our little cups or bowls.
Some people were just so hungry, that they, you know, rushed a line.
And they would try to scoop some soup out.
And they would absolutely be beaten to death.
I've seen people being beaten to death by trying to steal a little food.
So things got really, unbearably, very bad in Bergen-Belsen till liberation.
And people were very sick with typhoid, and dysentery, which is what I got too.
Were you staying with--
I had typhoid and dysentery both.
And--
Always.
Always, always.
You know, Anne Frank and I had very, very similar experiences after she left the hiding place and was taken to Germany.
As a matter of fact, I have a documentary here to show you that the Dutch government had come here, because the radio stations in Holland are run by the government.
So the government had called me after they read my book, and they realized that so many of our experiences were so similar.
And they asked if I would mind if they filmed me.
They wanted to make a documentary.
And just like you came today, they came right here in my house.
And they filmed me.
And they wanted me to continue the Anne Frank story that had stopped with the diary of what happened after we had arrived in Germany, when we got off the trains in Auschwitz, basically.
And you know, it so happened that after we had left Auschwitz, and the Franks had left Auschwitz, I mean, we stopped at Christianstadt.
But they went straight to Bergen-Belsen.
We arrived there at the same time in Bergen-Belsen.
And we were the same age, where she was one year older, but born June 12th, and I was born June 10, just one year apart.
She was one year older.
And she too had an older sister, Margo.
And the reason I'm mentioning this, you asked me did my sister survived too?
Or was I always with my sister?
They survived together.
And I honestly believe had Margo not gotten sick first and died, that Anne Frank would have survived.
But once Margo had died, she had completely given up all will to live.
There was nothing to live for once her sister died.
And she contacted typhoid, and died herself, just a few weeks before Bergen-Belsen was liberated.
So very often people will ask me, because of this similarity, you know, and I'm very active with the Anne Frank Center in New York.
If I had ever seen her there or known her, but, you know, that's such a difficult answer to give.
I always say probably not.
And yet, it's possible, because the young people like ourselves always stuck together.
We didn't want to be with the mothers, the older people.
Because they always talked about things that used to get us upset.
And they used to exchange recipes.
Here we were so hungry.
We were dying.
And they were giving each other recipes.
The older women, yeah.
And we tried to just kind of, you know, be with the younger people.
And we had our own things to talk about, mostly the future.
You know, we all talked about the future.
It's amazing, you know?
We talked about what we're going to go-- do when you go home, how we going to live differently, and how much we're going to eat.
We're going to eat all the time.
Oh, I thought I was going home to my own house.
Call me crazy.
But I really believed I was going home.
I was going to go home.
As a matter of fact, after we were liberated and I was so sick, that I was one of the first people to be taken to the hospital.
I was really moments from death.
I was so bad.
Because after they arrived, and they handed out some canned food, [DRINKS] some rations that they had with them, like Spam and stuff like that, army food, my sister had secured a can of food.
And she had taken a little tiny sharp rock, and a big rock, and she kind of worked it all around this can, you know, by hammering at it, and pried out the lid.
And she stuck her dirty finger into it, and she fed it to me.
Because she said, you're the one that's dying.
I want you to eat it.
So it would make you strong.

When this is very rich, greasy food went down my stomach, and I already had typhoid and dysentery, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got such diarrhea, that I couldn't even walk to the back of the barrack, or anything, you know?
I was just a mess.
So I went to hide in the barrack.
And my sister found me there later on, because first she was trying to talk to the liberators and get some information, and so forth.
But when she found me that bad, she saw that I was absolutely dying.
I mean, I was just really feverish and bad.
She dragged me outside the barracks so I would get some fresh air.
And she said, you stay here and don't move.
I'm going to go and get you some help.
And she went crying, yelling, my sister is dying.
Because she saw a Red Cross truck.
And she saw some guys with Red Cross armbands, you know, the liberators.
And she went.
And she dragged one of them over to me.
And he said-- she said, you must take her first, because she's dying.
So he took a red crayon and he marked my forehead, like a little cross on my-- on my forehead.
And as soon as he turned, he-- oh, so he said to my sister, don't worry.
She'll be one of the first ones we take.
I mean, I was already really like out.
And as soon he turned his head, she took some spittle, real quick, put it on my forehead, put her forehead on top of mine, like a blotter, lied down next to me.
And she said, I'm not gonna let them separate us now, after all that we've been together.
She says, I'm going with you when they come.
And sure enough, then they came, we got on the same stretcher.
And they took us to a hospital.
And I was unconscious for weeks there before, you know, they nursed me back.
Well, it was pretty much chaotic, except that they did have this roll call in the morning, cell appel.
And they collected the dead bodies.
And they made the prisoners collect them and take them, you know, to the piles.
And we still got that one meal.
But I told you, it was so sparse, that half the time the bucket wound up on the floor.
Nobody got any.
Because one person would rush it, you know.
And it was spilled.
And it was just horrendous.
Germans not care the last day.
No.
The night before, the very night before we were liberated, we heard rumors that, you know, we were going to be liberated.
And then as we were listening, we could hear gunshots.
And the gunshots were coming closer and closer.
And then we realized, OK, this is it.
You know.
Camp is going to be liberated.
The Russians must be close.
We didn't know it was the British.
Oh, yeah.
Well, as I said, I always thought I was going home.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After the liberation, I mean, it was before the liberation.
In the morning, the SS guards were moving away, but still shooting, still shooting, while they were leaving the camp and shooting back towards the camps.
They didn't want us to leave, I guess.
And some of the Germans actually tried to pretend they were prisoners.
They put on prisoner clothes.
But it was so ridiculous.
Here they were, big and fat, and we were more like shadows of human beings, you know?
And yet some of them tried to pass.
They had their head shaved.
And they put on our prisoner's uniform.
And they tried to pass as prisoners.
And then the following morning, there was no cell appel.
We saw this all the night before, the-- them trying to pass as prisoners, and shaving their heads.
You know, and our blokova helped them.
She actually helped them.
She was using this little clippers on their head and shaving them.
And they were laughing and giggling.
And we could hear them, you know, in her little partition.
And they were readying themselves to be prisoners.
And then the next morning, there was no cell appel.
There was no siren.
They weren't calling us.
So we figured they all left.
They deserted, you know?
So what did the people do?
They were so starved, they all started running for the kitchen to get some food.
And as they ran towards the kitchen, there were gunshots.
And a lot of them fell.
And my sister, of course, was one of them who was running.
So I said, OK.
That's-- that's how I'm going to lose her.
I'm going to lose her by a bullet now.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I saw people falling, you know?
But she had put me-- this was, of course, before I had that food, and before I passed out.
And she had put up against the wall in the sun, and she said, now you stay here.
[DRINKS] I'm going to get the-- some food.
But you stay here, and don't move.
And was she shot?
I think that we're supposed to stop now.
